metadata.subject: Physical and Health Education
metadata.description: Physical and Health Education 9 builds on the physical literacy and health foundations of earlier grades, challenging students to apply and refine movement skills across a wider range of activities, take greater ownership of personal fitness planning, and develop deeper health knowledge. Students engage with more complex health topics — including sexual health and relationships, psychoactive substances, and mental health challenges — and develop stronger critical thinking and advocacy skills. First Peoples perspectives on holistic well-being and community health continue to be integrated.
metadata.general_description: A comprehensive course refining physical literacy, deepening health knowledge, advancing fitness self-management, and developing critical thinking about health decisions — building personal and community health advocacy skills.

big_ideas.statement: Daily participation in different types of physical activity influences our physical literacy and personal health and fitness goals.
big_ideas.elaboration: Consistent, varied physical activity develops broader physical literacy and helps students identify activities they will value throughout their lives. At Grade 9, students begin taking more personal ownership of how and why they are active.

big_ideas.statement: Lifelong participation in physical activity has many benefits and is an essential part of a healthy lifestyle.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students deepen their understanding of the physical, mental, and social benefits of regular activity, and begin connecting current habits to long-term health trajectories.

big_ideas.statement: Healthy choices influence our physical, emotional, and mental well-being.
big_ideas.elaboration: Grade 9 students explore increasingly complex decisions about nutrition, substance use, sexual health, and mental wellness — developing the critical health literacy needed to navigate adolescent life.

big_ideas.statement: Healthy relationships can help us lead rewarding and fulfilling lives.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students examine the qualities of healthy and unhealthy relationships — including romantic and sexual relationships — and develop communication and boundary-setting skills appropriate to adolescence.

big_ideas.statement: Advocating for the health and well-being of others connects us to our community.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students develop the capacity to recognize health inequities, support peers in accessing help, and contribute to a healthy school and community culture.

competencies.raw: Develop, refine, and apply fundamental movement skills in various physical activities; develop and apply movement concepts and strategies in different activities; apply methods for monitoring and adjusting exertion levels; demonstrate safety, fair play, and leadership in physical activities; identify preferred types of physical activity.
i_can_statements: I can develop, refine, and apply locomotor, non-locomotor, and manipulative movement skills across a range of physical activities.
I can apply movement concepts — body, space, effort, and relationships — and tactical strategies to improve my performance in individual and team activities.
I can monitor my exertion levels using heart rate, RPE scale, or the talk test, and adjust my effort to meet activity goals.
I can demonstrate safety, fair play, and emerging leadership in physical activity settings.
I can identify and reflect on the types of physical activity I enjoy most and connect them to lifelong wellness.

competencies.raw: Participate daily in fitness-enhancing physical activity; describe how school, home, and community activities influence health; develop strategies for promoting healthy eating choices; assess factors influencing healthy choices; identify and analyze health message sources and their behavioural influence; identify and apply personal healthy-living goal strategies; reflect on outcomes and assess personal goal strategies.
i_can_statements: I can participate daily in physical activity that enhances my fitness and overall health.
I can describe how my activities at school, home, and in the community shape my health outcomes.
I can develop strategies for healthy eating and explain the factors — social, cultural, economic — that influence food choices.
I can critically analyze health messages from media, advertising, and social sources to evaluate their accuracy and intent.
I can set personal healthy-living goals, apply targeted strategies, and reflect honestly on my progress and outcomes.

competencies.raw: Propose strategies for avoiding and/or responding to unsafe or exploitative situations; propose strategies for responding to discrimination, stereotyping, and bullying; propose strategies for developing and maintaining healthy relationships; create strategies for promoting school and community well-being.
i_can_statements: I can propose effective strategies for recognizing and responding to unsafe, abusive, or exploitative situations — including online contexts.
I can propose thoughtful strategies for responding to and challenging discrimination, stereotyping, cyberbullying, and harassment.
I can develop and apply strategies for building and maintaining healthy, respectful, and consensual relationships.
I can design strategies and take action to promote the health and well-being of my school and broader community.

competencies.raw: Describe and assess strategies for promoting mental well-being; describe and assess strategies for managing mental health and substance use problems; create and assess strategies for managing physical, emotional, and social changes during puberty; explore and describe transition and change impacts on identities.
i_can_statements: I can describe and critically assess a range of strategies — including physical activity and social connection — for promoting mental well-being.
I can identify warning signs of mental health challenges and substance use issues, and assess strategies for seeking help and supporting peers.
I can create and evaluate personal strategies for navigating the physical, emotional, and social changes of adolescence.
I can explore how significant life transitions — puberty, school changes, identity development — affect sense of self and well-being.

areas.name: Fundamental Movement Skills and Physical Activity
areas.raw: Fundamental movement skills, movement concepts, physical activity types, and activity strategies.
topics: Non-locomotor skills: balance, rotation, weight transfer, and stabilization in activity contexts
Locomotor skills: applied running, jumping, and movement patterns in games and individual activities
Manipulative skills: refined throwing, catching, striking, and dribbling across activity types
Movement concepts: body, space, effort, and relationship concepts applied to performance improvement
Physical activity types: team sports, individual/dual activities, outdoor pursuits, rhythmic and expressive activities
Tactical strategies: positioning, decision-making, and teamwork in game-based activities

areas.name: Fitness and Training
areas.raw: FITT, SAID principles, fitness monitoring, goal-setting, and training effects.
topics: Components of fitness: cardiovascular endurance, muscular strength, muscular endurance, flexibility, body composition
FITT principle: Frequency, Intensity, Type, Time — applied to personal fitness program design
SAID principle: Specific Adaptation to Imposed Demand — understanding how training produces specific results
Monitoring exertion: heart rate (resting, target, and maximum), RPE scale, and talk test
Effects of training: physiological adaptations in muscles, cardiovascular system, and flexibility
SMART goal-setting: applying specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound goals to fitness

areas.name: Healthy Living and Decision Making
areas.raw: Nutrition, sexual health, substance use, safety, and health information literacy.
topics: Nutrition: macronutrients and micronutrients, energy balance, nutritional myths, and food labelling
Sexual health: adolescent development, sexual decision-making, consent, protection methods, and STI basics
Intimate partner violence: recognizing unhealthy and abusive relationship patterns and seeking help
Psychoactive substances: effects of alcohol, tobacco, cannabis, and other drugs on the developing brain
Substance use: decision-making under social pressure, refusal skills, and harm reduction awareness
Health information literacy: evaluating credibility of health sources — media, social media, professionals
Emergency response: expanded first aid including choking, bleeding, and when to call for help
Online safety: privacy, digital consent, sexting, and recognizing online exploitation

areas.name: Social and Community Health
areas.raw: Relationships, bullying, discrimination, community well-being, and First Peoples perspectives.
topics: Healthy and unhealthy relationships: communication, respect, boundaries, and consent in peer and romantic contexts
Bullying and cyberbullying: recognizing patterns, bystander roles, and effective response strategies
Discrimination and stereotyping: bias based on race, gender, sexual orientation, disability, and religion
Intimate partner relationships: recognizing unhealthy dynamics and accessing support
Peer pressure and social influences: developing personal values and assertiveness
Community well-being: peer support, inclusion, and advocacy for health equity
First Peoples perspectives on relational health and community responsibility

areas.name: Mental Well-being
areas.raw: Mental health, adolescent changes, stress, identity, and resilience.
topics: Mental health continuum: understanding the range from flourishing to mental illness
Adolescent brain development: how the developing brain affects mood, risk-taking, and decision-making
Stress and anxiety: physiological responses, coping strategies, and when to seek help
Depression and mood: recognizing symptoms, de-stigmatizing help-seeking, and supporting peers
Identity development: exploring gender identity, sexual orientation, culture, and self-concept
Resilience strategies: emotional regulation, mindfulness, problem-solving, and self-compassion
Physical activity and mental health: the relationship between exercise, mood, and cognitive function
